ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for vegetarian kofta curry. FOODON_03315595 (curry dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Vegetable dishes) is the closest parent. No specific kofta curry code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to vegetarian kofta curry. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Nasrati Kofta Salan is approximately 300 g, comprising 4–6 fried kofta balls in a generous cashew-based gravy, served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Malai Kofta (vegetarian fried dumpling curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central component data for paneer, spinach, potato, and cashew
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct ICMR-NIN entry exists for Nasrati Kofta Salan. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for Malai Kofta (the closest analogous North Indian vegetarian kofta curry) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for spinach (FDC 168462), paneer (FDC 2003588), potato (FDC 170026), and cashew (FDC 170162). The cashew-cream gravy increases fat and energy relative to a plain vegetable curry. Estimated per-100g values reflect a cooked dish with kofta and gravy in approximately 40:60 ratio.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry dishes — analogous estimate based on potato-based vegetable curry (GI ~45) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasrati Kofta Salan. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the closest analogous food: potato-based mixed vegetable curry with legume/dairy components, as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. The presence of fat (cashew, cream) and protein (paneer) in the dish further attenuates glycemic response relative to plain potato. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 11.6 g carbohydrate per 100g × 300g serving) / 100 = 15.7, rounded to 16.1 accounting for the full 300g portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100g; ~930 mg per 300g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300g restaurant serving provides approximately 930 mg sodium (~40% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes such as pickles or pappadum.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from potato kofta; attenuated by fat and protein from cashew and paneer
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~45) and moderate glycemic load (~16 per serving) make this dish manageable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (150–200g), paired with a high-fibre bread or salad, and without added refined-carbohydrate sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from paneer and cream (~3.4g per 100g; ~10.2g per serving) and deep-frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from deep-frying, paneer, and cream-based gravy contributes meaningfully to daily saturated fat intake; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption and request preparation with reduced cream and shallow-frying of kofta where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasrati Kofta Salan (Vegetarian) is a rich North Indian / Mughlai-style curry dish consisting of round, deep-fried dumplings (kofta) made from mashed spinach, paneer (Indian cottage cheese), and potato, served in a smooth, aromatic cashew-nut and tomato-based gravy. The kofta are golden and slightly crisp on the outside with a soft, savoury interior. The gravy is creamy, mildly spiced, and nutty in flavour, typically seasoned with cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, and garlic. The dish is fully vegetarian and is commonly served at Indian restaurants in Singapore, including at establishments specialising in North Indian and Mughlai cuisine. Nutritionally, it is moderately energy-dense (~148 kcal per 100g) with notable fat content from deep-frying and cashew cream, moderate protein from paneer, and a low glycemic index (~45) due to the fat-protein matrix attenuating carbohydrate absorption. The sodium content is clinically relevant for hypertension management. It provides calcium and iron from paneer and spinach respectively, and vitamin A from spinach. This dish is suitable for vegetarians and is typically halal and gluten-free. It is often paired with naan, roti, or basmati rice.

meal_context: paired with naan or basmati rice

confidence_reason: No direct ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Nasrati Kofta Salan specifically. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest analogous dish (Malai Kofta) in ICMR-NIN and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-documented and the analogy to Malai Kofta is strong.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Malai Kofta data and USDA FoodData Central component data; a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish would improve confidence to high. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA component sources for this dish type. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish type. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~45) based on potato-based vegetable curry with fat-protein attenuation; no direct GI measurement for Nasrati Kofta Salan was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA component data for the constituent ingredients (cashew, paneer, frying oil). 6. The dish is served at Briyani Hut, Boat Quay, Singapore (GrabFood source); restaurant-specific preparation may vary in fat and sodium content. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the vegetarian ingredient profile; formal halal certification of the specific restaurant has not been verified.
